stories.headline: ChatGPT now has a lockdown mode to stop prompt injection leaks
stories.blurb: OpenAI launched Lockdown Mode to protect sensitive data when ChatGPT gets tricked by hidden instructions in documents or websites. It won't stop all prompt injections, but it's designed to prevent your private data from leaking when an attack succeeds.
stories.why_it_matters: Prompt injection is still an unsolved problem, but now there's a guard rail for your data.
stories.original_headline: OpenAI unveils Lockdown Mode to protect sensitive data from prompt injection attacks

stories.headline: Google is paying SpaceX nearly $1 billion a month for cloud computing
stories.blurb: SpaceX signed a deal with Google Cloud worth close to $1 billion per month, creating a major new revenue stream for the rocket company's AI infrastructure business as it prepares for an IPO. The agreement marks one of the largest cloud computing contracts in recent years.
stories.why_it_matters: SpaceX is now a serious player in enterprise AI infrastructure, not just rockets
stories.original_headline: Google to Pay SpaceX Nearly $1 Billion a Month in Cloud-Computing Deal

stories.headline: Cursor's design mode now lets you sketch UI changes with your mouse
stories.blurb: Cursor's browser-based design mode now accepts clicks, drawn sketches, or voice commands to tell its AI agent how to modify your UI. You can literally draw on a webpage to show what you want changed, and the agent updates the code.
stories.why_it_matters: Natural input methods — sketches, clicks, voice — are replacing written prompts for UI work.

stories.headline: Apple is about to relaunch Siri (again) at WWDC
stories.blurb: After years of falling behind in AI, Apple is set to unveil yet another Siri redesign at Monday's WWDC keynote. The Verge notes that playing catch-up might actually work in Apple's favor this time, as the company can learn from competitors' missteps before committing to a strategy.
stories.why_it_matters: Apple's slow-and-steady approach could mean a more polished AI assistant when it finally ships

stories.headline: China is training home robots by filming people fold laundry
stories.blurb: Chinese AI companies are collecting robotics training data directly in homes and small factories, recording ordinary people doing household tasks like folding clothes and washing dishes. The approach costs less and scales faster than the U.S. model of lab research and outsourced annotation, giving China an edge in teaching robots to handle real-world messiness.
stories.why_it_matters: Whoever collects the best mundane-task data first will likely dominate the home robotics market.
stories.original_headline: China is training a robot future — one folded shirt at a time Localized, low-cost data harvested in homes and factories gives China a scaling edge over the research-heavy and outsourced U.S. approach.

try_this_prompt.title: The confidence audit
try_this_prompt.prompt: I'm about to paste something I wrote that I'm weirdly proud of. Don't compliment it. Tell me which sentence I'm hiding behind, which claim has no evidence, and which paragraph exists only because I enjoyed writing it. Then show me the same idea in half the words.
try_this_prompt.tool_hint: Before you send the deck, the doc, or the all-hands email you spent too long on.

daily_question: If you could teach a home robot one household task right now, what would it be?
